notes: From: Voters Are Worried About Crypto’s Impacts on Local Economies and Politics

A new Data for Progress survey finds that a majority of voters (63%) have heard, seen, or read “a lot” about recent plane crashes in the U.S., while only 4% have heard “nothing at all.” A majority of voters (75%) also say they are either “very concerned” or “somewhat concerned” about aviation safety in the U.S.

question_wording: In general, swing voters consume traditional news sources, such as Fox News and CNN, considerably less than likely voters overall, but consume some forms of social media, such as Instagram and TikTok, at a similar rate (only options selected by at least 10% of respondents are shown in the chart below).

question_wording: Views on Harris and Trump on the Key Issues By a large margin, the top issue for swing voters this election is the economy, jobs, and inflation (30%), followed by programs like Social Security and Medicare (11%) and immigration (10%).

question_wording: Consistent with likely voters overall, swing voters’ concern about the economy is overwhelmingly focused on inflation (63%), and those concerned about inflation are particularly concerned about the cost of food and groceries (62%).

question_wording: While voters overall favor stable leadership over significant changes by a 64% to 32% margin, swing voters are more split — with 49% selecting stable leadership and 42% selecting significant changes.

question_wording: On average, nearly half (47%) of swing voters say they don’t know which candidate these statements best apply to.
notes: From: Decision Time: A Final Look at the Swing Voters Who Could Decide the Election

question_wording: The three statements that swing voters feel most confident assessing — which candidate is more extreme (31% don’t know), which is more dangerous (34% don’t know), and which is more experienced (35% don’t know) — are all qualities that they more closely associate with Trump.

question_wording: When asked whether they’re more concerned about Trump’s extremism or Harris’ liberal ideology, swing voters say they’re more concerned about Trump’s extremism (45%) than Harris’ liberal ideology (36%).

notes: From: Powering the Future: Voters Back Renewable Energy Investment Despite Divides Over Costs and Grid Reliability

notes: From: Powering the Future: Voters Back Renewable Energy Investment Despite Divides Over Costs and Grid Reliability

notes: From: Powering the Future: Voters Back Renewable Energy Investment Despite Divides Over Costs and Grid Reliability

question_wording: Measuring the Current: 77% of Voters Agree More Transmission Is Important By Eva Brungard To unlock the renewable energy potential of the Inflation Reduction Act, the build-out of transmission infrastructure is key.
notes: From: Measuring the Current: 77% of Voters Agree More Transmission Is Important

question_wording: An overwhelming, bipartisan majority of voters (77%) believe that building more transmission is important for the U.S.
notes: From: Measuring the Current: 77% of Voters Agree More Transmission Is Important

question_wording: When asked about overhead transmission lines being built in their community, more than a third of respondents (34%) have not heard enough to say, with the remainder of respondents split between a favorable opinion (30%) and an unfavorable opinion (36%).
notes: From: Measuring the Current: 77% of Voters Agree More Transmission Is Important

question_wording: Unsurprisingly, underground lines are more favorable (52%) despite being cost prohibitive for developers to date, though 34% of voters still haven’t heard enough to say their opinions.
notes: From: Measuring the Current: 77% of Voters Agree More Transmission Is Important

question_wording: As a whole, underground infrastructure projects are perceived more favorably by voters than those which have a visible footprint, with the exception of solar panel farms (63% favorable).
notes: From: Measuring the Current: 77% of Voters Agree More Transmission Is Important

question_wording: In general, the vast majority of American voters (90%) have heard little to nothing at all about transmission lines.
notes: From: Measuring the Current: 77% of Voters Agree More Transmission Is Important

question_wording: Indeed, after reading a description of transmission lines that included information about their role in the electric power system and noted that these lines are typically placed overhead, 55% of voters say they would support a transmission line being built in their community.
notes: From: Measuring the Current: 77% of Voters Agree More Transmission Is Important

question_wording: Support is slightly lower among Black (49%) voters, compared with white (57%) and Latino (66%) voters.
notes: From: Measuring the Current: 77% of Voters Agree More Transmission Is Important

question_wording: A majority of respondents (60%) agree that new transmission would improve reliability, leading to fewer blackouts and outages.
notes: From: Measuring the Current: 77% of Voters Agree More Transmission Is Important

question_wording: When asked about the impact of transmission build-out on their electricity bills, a plurality of voters (41%) believe that transmission projects would increase rates, compared with 23% who expect they would decrease rates.
notes: From: Measuring the Current: 77% of Voters Agree More Transmission Is Important

question_wording: Most voters (58%) prefer a publicly owned and operated transmission system, compared with just 24% who support private ownership.
notes: From: Measuring the Current: 77% of Voters Agree More Transmission Is Important

question_wording: The support for public ownership holds across party lines, with Democrats (61%), Independents (53%) and Republicans (58%) all in support of publicly owned transmission.
notes: From: Measuring the Current: 77% of Voters Agree More Transmission Is Important

question_wording: Voters most want landowners to be compensated by recurring lease payments for the land (33%), followed by recurring royalty payments based on the revenue generated by the transmission lines (27%).
notes: From: Measuring the Current: 77% of Voters Agree More Transmission Is Important

question_wording: Just 15% of voters support a one-time, lump sum payment for landowners, suggesting that, overall, voters want to see ongoing compensation for the impacts that landowners bear due to transmission projects.
notes: From: Measuring the Current: 77% of Voters Agree More Transmission Is Important

question_wording: Voters most want a new transmission line to lower their energy bills (56%), followed by making the grid more reliable (50%) and enabling clean energy (43%).
notes: From: Measuring the Current: 77% of Voters Agree More Transmission Is Important

question_wording: Very few respondents cite the need for a new transmission line not to ruin their view (15%) or take up a small amount of land (16%) as important for securing their support.
notes: From: Measuring the Current: 77% of Voters Agree More Transmission Is Important

question_wording: Below are the key findings from this research: Latino Voters Are Motivated to Elect a President Who Will Take Extreme Weather and Climate Change Seriously and Hold Big Oil Polluters Accountable Latino voters prioritize addressing climate change and extreme weather, with 65% of respondents agreeing with a statement saying the U.S.
notes: From: Latino Voters Want a President Who Will Address Costs, Causes of Extreme Weather

question_wording: needs a president committed to addressing both issues and making them a key priority of their policy agenda, including 53% of Latino voters in battleground states, 56% of Latino Independents, and two-thirds of Latino voters under age 45.
notes: From: Latino Voters Want a President Who Will Address Costs, Causes of Extreme Weather

question_wording: In contrast, just 30% of respondents overall agree with a statement saying that we need a president who is committed to expanding oil and gas production and pushing back against a climate alarmist policy agenda.
notes: From: Latino Voters Want a President Who Will Address Costs, Causes of Extreme Weather

question_wording: Latino Voters Worry About the Impacts of Extreme Weather and the Financial Toll It Takes on Their Communities Over three-quarters of Latino voters (78%) are at least somewhat concerned about themselves or someone in their family being affected by an extreme weather event, like a heat wave or flood.
notes: From: Latino Voters Want a President Who Will Address Costs, Causes of Extreme Weather

question_wording: This includes majorities of Latino voters in key swing states (73%), as well as Latino voters who identify as Democrats (86%), Independents (71%), and Republicans (70%).
notes: From: Latino Voters Want a President Who Will Address Costs, Causes of Extreme Weather

question_wording: Over three-quarters of Latino voters report that extreme weather events impact their energy bills (78%) and electricity bills (78%), highlighting that Latino voters think extreme weather causes similar levels of impact, regardless of which term is used.
notes: From: Latino Voters Want a President Who Will Address Costs, Causes of Extreme Weather

question_wording: In addition to a general increase in energy costs, Latino voters report home heating and cooling costs (77%) and their ability to enjoy the outdoors (74%) are also somewhat or greatly impacted by extreme weather.
notes: From: Latino Voters Want a President Who Will Address Costs, Causes of Extreme Weather

question_wording: Latino voters have already faced additional costs due to extreme weather events in the last five years, with a strong majority reporting they’ve paid more for electricity bills (79%), food and grocery prices (78%), home heating and cooling costs (75%), gas prices (74%), and energy bills (71%).
notes: From: Latino Voters Want a President Who Will Address Costs, Causes of Extreme Weather

question_wording: Strong majorities of Latino voters say they expect extreme weather events to lead to rising costs of home heating and cooling (89%), and rising energy (88%) and electricity bills (87%), in addition to rising costs of groceries and gas (both at 86%).
notes: From: Latino Voters Want a President Who Will Address Costs, Causes of Extreme Weather

question_wording: Latino voters hold oil and gas companies (53%) mostresponsible, and this finding holds across nearly all demographic subgroups, including partisanship, age, gender, and education.
notes: From: Latino Voters Want a President Who Will Address Costs, Causes of Extreme Weather

question_wording: Moreover, Data for Progress also finds 41% of Latino voters say climate change is playing a more important factor in their vote in 2024, relative to the 2020 election.
notes: From: Latino Voters Want a President Who Will Address Costs, Causes of Extreme Weather

question_wording: From the beginning of the year to when Biden decided to drop out, Harris was viewed favorably by an average of 41% of voters and unfavorably by an average of 54%.
notes: From: Vibe Check: Voter Enthusiasm and Harris’ Favorability Have Surged Since Biden’s Exit

question_wording: Since July 21, her average favorability rating has become net-positive, with 50% viewing her favorably and 48% viewing her unfavorably.
notes: From: Vibe Check: Voter Enthusiasm and Harris’ Favorability Have Surged Since Biden’s Exit

question_wording: Since the beginning of the year, Biden’s favorability rating has averaged 43% and Trump’s has averaged 44%, with little variation.
notes: From: Vibe Check: Voter Enthusiasm and Harris’ Favorability Have Surged Since Biden’s Exit

question_wording: Across these two surveys, an average of 39% of voters — including 40% of Democrats, 67% of Independents who lean toward Democrats, and 50% of Independents who don’t lean toward either party — selected “I wish we had different choices.” Data for Progress asked the same question again in two surveys conducted after Biden dropped out.
notes: From: Vibe Check: Voter Enthusiasm and Harris’ Favorability Have Surged Since Biden’s Exit

question_wording: Across these surveys, the percentage of voters selecting that they wish we had different choices drops to 25%, including only 16% of Democrats, 31% of Independents who lean toward Democrats, and 40% of Independents who don’t lean toward either party.
notes: From: Vibe Check: Voter Enthusiasm and Harris’ Favorability Have Surged Since Biden’s Exit

question_wording: In the first two surveys, an average of 20% of voters selected “I am excited,” including 19% of Democrats and 5% of Independents who lean toward Democrats.
notes: From: Vibe Check: Voter Enthusiasm and Harris’ Favorability Have Surged Since Biden’s Exit

question_wording: In the two surveys conducted after Biden’s exit, the percentage of voters selecting that they feel excited about the election rises to an average of 25%, including 34% of Democrats and 27% of Independents who lean toward Democrats.
notes: From: Vibe Check: Voter Enthusiasm and Harris’ Favorability Have Surged Since Biden’s Exit

question_wording: In a poll conducted in early July, 53% of voters said they thought Trump would beat Biden in a head-to-head matchup, while only 39% said Biden would win.
notes: From: Vibe Check: Voter Enthusiasm and Harris’ Favorability Have Surged Since Biden’s Exit

question_wording: However, a survey completed after Biden’s exit finds 46% of voters think Harris will beat Trump, compared with 44% who say Trump will win.
notes: From: Vibe Check: Voter Enthusiasm and Harris’ Favorability Have Surged Since Biden’s Exit

question_wording: The survey finds Walz has net-positive favorability (35% favorable, 32% unfavorable) while Vance has net-negative favorability (35% favorable, 43% unfavorable).
notes: From: Vibe Check: Voter Enthusiasm and Harris’ Favorability Have Surged Since Biden’s Exit

question_wording: The survey finds that more than three-quarters of likely voters (80%) would approve of Harris’ hypothetical decision to support requiring social media companies to protect children’s safety and privacy online.
notes: From: Likely Voters Would Approve if Kamala Harris Held Big Tech Companies Accountable

question_wording: Support for this decision is shared by voters across party lines, as 96% of Democrats, 78% of Independents, and 64% of Republicans say they would approve.
notes: From: Likely Voters Would Approve if Kamala Harris Held Big Tech Companies Accountable

question_wording: A strong majority of voters (78%) would approve of Harris’ hypothetical decision to support requiring online platforms to protect their users’ personal health information.
notes: From: Likely Voters Would Approve if Kamala Harris Held Big Tech Companies Accountable

question_wording: The survey finds that 72% of voters would approve if Harris decided to enforce consumer protection laws against AI companies, including 91% of Democrats, 71% of Independents, and 53% of Republicans.
notes: From: Likely Voters Would Approve if Kamala Harris Held Big Tech Companies Accountable

question_wording: A strong majority of voters (70%) would approve if Harris decided to support reforming Section 230.
notes: From: Likely Voters Would Approve if Kamala Harris Held Big Tech Companies Accountable

question_wording: Data for Progress conducted a new survey to gauge attitudes toward PFAS, finding that 71% of likely voters have heard nothing at all about the term.
notes: From: Voters Are Deeply Concerned About Health Impacts of PFAS and Support Creating a Fund for Groups Impacted by Exposure

question_wording: The related term “forever chemicals” is still unfamiliar to 50% of voters, demonstrating that the salience of these issues remains low overall.
notes: From: Voters Are Deeply Concerned About Health Impacts of PFAS and Support Creating a Fund for Groups Impacted by Exposure

question_wording: After reading this, a strong bipartisan majority of respondents (72%) say that the federal government is not providing enough access to information and resources on PFAS.
notes: From: Voters Are Deeply Concerned About Health Impacts of PFAS and Support Creating a Fund for Groups Impacted by Exposure

question_wording: When asked what, if any, health problems they associate with PFAS or forever chemicals, respondents most commonly cite kidney, liver, prostate, and testicular cancer (34%).
notes: From: Voters Are Deeply Concerned About Health Impacts of PFAS and Support Creating a Fund for Groups Impacted by Exposure

question_wording: Respondents also associate PFAS with developmental effects (33%), followed closely by reproductive harm (32%), and lower immune system response (29%).
notes: From: Voters Are Deeply Concerned About Health Impacts of PFAS and Support Creating a Fund for Groups Impacted by Exposure

question_wording: Nearly 1 in 5 respondents (18%) say they do not associate any of the health problems listed with PFAS, and over a quarter say they have not heard of either PFAS or forever chemicals (26%).
notes: From: Voters Are Deeply Concerned About Health Impacts of PFAS and Support Creating a Fund for Groups Impacted by Exposure

question_wording: Three-quarters (75%) of respondents say they support the new PFAS drinking water standards, with the rule receiving strong bipartisan support.
notes: From: Voters Are Deeply Concerned About Health Impacts of PFAS and Support Creating a Fund for Groups Impacted by Exposure

question_wording: Moreover, around one-third of voters say that industry and chemical companies should be held most responsible for paying to clean up municipal water systems to meet the new drinking water standards (36%).
notes: From: Voters Are Deeply Concerned About Health Impacts of PFAS and Support Creating a Fund for Groups Impacted by Exposure

question_wording: On average, respondents estimate that around 65% of Americans have PFAS in their bloodstream.
notes: From: Voters Are Deeply Concerned About Health Impacts of PFAS and Support Creating a Fund for Groups Impacted by Exposure

question_wording: Later, respondents read that an estimated 98% of Americans have some level of PFAS in their bloodstream, that some forms of PFAS can take several years to exit the body, and that PFAS exposure is linked to serious health issues, including cancers and hormone disruption.
notes: From: Voters Are Deeply Concerned About Health Impacts of PFAS and Support Creating a Fund for Groups Impacted by Exposure

question_wording: More than 8 in 10 voters (83%) respond that they are concerned about long-term exposure to PFAS, with over half saying that they are very concerned (51%).
notes: From: Voters Are Deeply Concerned About Health Impacts of PFAS and Support Creating a Fund for Groups Impacted by Exposure

question_wording: More than three-quarters of respondents (78%) report that they are at least somewhat concerned about their own exposure to PFAS in the food supply.
notes: From: Voters Are Deeply Concerned About Health Impacts of PFAS and Support Creating a Fund for Groups Impacted by Exposure

question_wording: Voters are most concerned about PFAS exposure via direct consumption, including through drinking water (69%) and food or food packaging (56%).
notes: From: Voters Are Deeply Concerned About Health Impacts of PFAS and Support Creating a Fund for Groups Impacted by Exposure

question_wording: Notably, among the listed routes of exposure, voters express the least concern about firefighting foam, also known as AFFF (5%), despite the fact that AFFF is one of the largest sources of PFAS contamination in drinking water.
notes: From: Voters Are Deeply Concerned About Health Impacts of PFAS and Support Creating a Fund for Groups Impacted by Exposure

question_wording: Similarly, few respondents (9%) share concerns about exposure from biosolids, often broadly referred to as municipal sludge, which is a significant carrier of PFAS.
notes: From: Voters Are Deeply Concerned About Health Impacts of PFAS and Support Creating a Fund for Groups Impacted by Exposure

question_wording: Among voters who indicate they would support the establishment of a fund for groups adversely impacted by PFAS contamination, 60% think that both federal and state governments should be responsible for creating such a fund.
notes: From: Voters Are Deeply Concerned About Health Impacts of PFAS and Support Creating a Fund for Groups Impacted by Exposure

question_wording: About a quarter of voters (27%) think the federal government alone should be responsible for creating a fund, while 12% think only individual state governments should be responsible.
notes: From: Voters Are Deeply Concerned About Health Impacts of PFAS and Support Creating a Fund for Groups Impacted by Exposure

question_wording: In both cases, fewer than 1 in 10 (8%) voters have heard, seen, or read “a lot.” One pernicious form of mistreatment and discrimination against the dwarfism community is the practice of “dwarf tossing,” a bar sport in which bar patrons try to see who can throw a little person the farthest.
notes: From: Voters Are Largely Unaware of the Discrimination Faced by Little People

question_wording: When voters are informed of this practice, and told that some lawmakers are considering making dwarf tossing illegal, a bipartisan majority of voters (58%) say they support banning dwarf tossing.

question_wording: Even after being presented with arguments in favor of and against dwarf tossing, a majority of voters (55%), including a majority of Democrats (64%) and pluralities of both Independents (49%) and Republicans (49%), support banning dwarf tossing.
notes: From: Voters Are Largely Unaware of the Discrimination Faced by Little People
